issues.title: How to queue CE marked packets
issues.author: lcolitti
issues.body: The text currently says that CE marked packets MUST be put in the low-latency queue. Is this correct? Doesn't that mean that potentially queue-building Classic ECN traffic might be put in the low-latency queue, and potentially starve that queue?

issues.title: Check the text for per-packet ECN controls for multiplexed sockets
issues.author: fridek
issues.body: Sockets that multiplex multiple traffic types (e.g., WebRTC multiplexing RTP media, RTCP control, and SCTP data channels on a single UDP socket) cannot use simple socket-wide setsockopt(IP_TOS) because some flows (like SCTP) are queue-building and must not be marked ECT(1).

issues.title: Consider citing RFC 9557
issues.author: lcolitti
issues.body: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/rfc9957/ describes an algorithm to eject non-L4S traffic from the low-latency queue. Should we say that link layers SHOULD (or MAY) implement this algorithm?

issues.title: Align with RFC 9332: Dual-Queue Coupled AQM for L4S
issues.author: fridek

comments.author: Irlanki
comments.body: Added https://github.com/fridek/draft-sporeba-tsvwg-mobile-l4s/pull/12 to address this issue. Please check and suggest modifications.

issues.title: Provide operational guidelines for the low-latency queue thresholds for marking and hard drop limits
issues.author: fridek
issues.body:
[Ingemar expressed](https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/tsvwg/ayzffQ_WNefOc3kcvWeZ9qZXD7M/) that "it is not possible to state any clear values for the L4S marking thresholds." - my understanding of your argument is that different configurations (like a 5G RAN) would benefit from different thresholds and therefore we can't come up with one number. I think I could address that while remaining useful, by providing a guideline for what would be realistic and appropriate for mobile subsystems only (modem, wifi, etc.)
[Sebastian writes](https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/tsvwg/EUf9b2qMHv1_ZjHM0kbuH8GoUPY/) "should that size be in fixed packets, fixed aggregate bytes or in expected time to service?" I believe we would want to express it as sojourn time in the queue. There exist configurations which will write packets in a larger bulk (resulting in 100s of kB chunk burst) . There also exist less popular configurations like interfaces over USB where the IP packet size can be up to 64kB. This makes settling on a size-based boundary difficult.

comments.author: Irlanki
comments.body: Added https://github.com/fridek/draft-sporeba-tsvwg-mobile-l4s/pull/12 to address this issue. Please check and suggest modifications.

issues.title: Decide on the scoping of the document
issues.author: fridek
issues.body:
[Kevin pointed out](https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/tsvwg/VIXDXiMbgTxW-eMvSnDFTNpTvcg/) that on-path requirements hardly belong in a BCP on mobile devices. My intent was to cover all the subsystems that are unique to mobile devices - system, link-layer subsystems, and what I personally think of as the "last mile" of the internet access - ISPs and carriers, with a lot of specific hardware unique to the mobile ecosystem.
Conversely, my view is that I shouldn't overspecify what the application-layer stacks do (since they will, based on my experience, do anything and everything technically permitted). These requirements are also covered well in other documents. I kept the scope of what's needed to enable correct L4S implementation in UDP-based stacks (like QUIC).
Overall I'm more keen on cutting the scope to mobile device components only, or extracting it to another draft than expanding to applications.

issues.title: Consider the details of out-of-order packet delivery statement
issues.author: fridek
issues.body:
[Ingemar points out](https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/tsvwg/ayzffQ_WNefOc3kcvWeZ9qZXD7M/) the work on [draft-white-intarea-reordering](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-white-intarea-reordering/) and that 4G spec requires in-order delivery while 5G spec makes it optional.
We want to make a normative statement on what's needed for optimal operation in a mobile device context

pulls.title: Add transport feedback requirements (RFC9768 and RFC6679)
pulls.author: Irlanki
pulls.body:
Added a new subsection (## Transport-Layer Congestion Feedback Requirements) under the Host OS section.

This addresses endpoint pseudo-L4S deployments by strictly requiring active, protocol-compliant congestion loop closure.

comments.body: I think this would fit well into section 4 on the middleboxes, which I have left low on such detail. Section 2 covers Host system requiremets (i.e. mobile operating system).

Perhaps we could write more generally: 

"Devices that do not implement queueing feedback by marking the ECN codepoints in line with RFC 9768 MUST NOT act on the `ECT(1)` flags by prioritising the traffic in alternative, non-compliant ways." 

I believe there are some sections already describing the non-compliance scenarios, such as rfc3168 section 7 and 8 and rfc 9786 section 3.3. I don't think anyone predicted what we see in practice - that deployments might be marketed as L4S without implementing L4S.
This is minor can we check AccECN is "MUST" and not "SHOULD"? Is using only classic ECN considering a compliant implementation?

It is a strict MUST for L4S compliance. Classic ECN feedback is not considered a compliant implementation for an L4S endpoint because it cannot provide the high-fidelity signaling required by a Scalable congestion control algorithm.

Per the underlying specifications:

RFC 9331, Section 4.2 (Prerequisite Transport Feedback): Explicitly states that for a transport protocol to provide Scalable congestion control, it MUST provide feedback of the accurate extent of CE marking on the forward path. For TCP, it explicitly notes that support for accurate ECN feedback requirements is a prerequisite.

RFC 9768, Section 3.2.2 (The ACE Field): Formally mandates the packet-based loop mechanics, stating that a Data Receiver MUST encode the 3 least significant bits of its packet-based CE counter (r.cep) into the overloaded 3-bit ACE field in the main TCP header.

(Note: In contrast, Section 3.2.3 of RFC 9768 specifies that the byte-counting AccECN TCP Options are supplementary and remain optional).

Acknowledged. Feedback is strictly an endpoint responsibility, which is why I originally put it in the host section.

However, since this rule acts as a direct constraint on how the network handles traffic, it makes sense to place it in the Middleboxes section also. I have updated and consolidated the text there.

Thanks, Rephrased the text as per your suggestion. I also expanded it slightly to explicitly bring in the core transport protocols (TCP, UDP/RTP, and QUIC) under the blanket prerequisite loop rules defined in RFC 9331 Section 4.2, and added specific section references for their respective feedback expectations (such as  per-packet metric format in RFC 8888).

Thinking about this further, I still felt that host requirements belong strictly in the host section, since "host" refers to endpoints like devices or servers that manage transport stacks.

To keep the architecture clean, I separated the text into two distinct sections:

The middlebox constraints stay in the Middlebox section.

The specific endpoint protocol requirements (TCP, UDP, QUIC) are placed under the Host section.

I then added an explicit cross-reference from the middlebox section linking back to the host prerequisites. This keeps the separation of concerns perfect. Please check the latest push
It feels like we should say that hosts SHOULD (not MAY) implement AccECN, and why.

pulls.title: Specify the on-path devices we are concerned with. Apply minor improvements
pulls.author: fridek
pulls.body: I have removed the section on server requirements, and added an paragraph on scoping - omitting anything beyond a carrier or ISP network. This is also to reflect the conversation we had on scoping. I also think that the detection of end-to-end L4S support is impractical, and likely won't get traction.

comments.body: Looks fine. RFC8888, RFC6679, RFC9000 can be removed from normative as these are no longer referenced.

pulls.title: Incorporate some of the tsvwg feedback
pulls.author: fridek

pulls.title: Add sojourn metrics, dual-tier thresholds, and flow isolation parameters
pulls.author: Irlanki

comments.body:
Are these new normative statements or reiteration of other text?

There is a notes section in 9332 A.1 which starts with "sojourn time is slow to detect bursts" which describes bursty traffic sojourn time spreading recommendation - is this what you are referring to?
I'm not sure I understand this statement. What are the implications of this number?
